title: Hermes Bible: You Can Copy REAL Agent Workflows
summary_verdict: Hermes Bible is a high-signal, community-built workflow index worth bookmarking today, but treat its loop outputs as drafts and add your own QC agent before trusting them.
summary_key_takeaways: Submit your own production workflow to the Hermes Bible — the knowledge base grows from user contributions, so adding yours compounds the index for power users.
Configure `agents.md` and `user.md` before anything else; if you are stuck at level 0 or 1 on the 15-level roadmap, a missing context file is almost always the cause, not a bad prompt.
Run `tmux` inside a fresh project directory and pair it with `/goal` to build a loop that survives VPS disconnects — the Bible's hidden-features page skips this entirely.

Never trust a `/goal` loop output without a second agent for fact-checking and QC; treat loop output as a draft, especially the 21 copy-paste commands across research, lead gen, content, email, operations, and dev.
Route code refactoring to `Claude Code`, `Kimi Code`, or `Kilo Code` instead of `/goal` — use Hermes as the orchestrator that delegates to those harnesses, not as the coder.
Use the `Grok` + Hermes workflow for X research if you have X premium, not the X API, to avoid API billing while still pulling trending posts.
Skip loops for one-off creative work and reserve them for recurring system checks and timed research digests — the `kanban dispatcher` and `memory` loops are the highest-leverage starters.

title: Advanced Loops in Claude Code (Our Setup)
summary_verdict: Loop engineering with Claude Code works, but it's a mindset shift — humans think linearly while agents thrive on continuous loops, so start with measurable tasks (perf, scraping) before tackling subjective ones like content review.
summary_key_takeaways: Use the `/loop` command in Claude Code instead of hand-rolling loop scripts — pass it the interval, prompt, and setup.
Start with a performance loop on a slow dashboard running every 10–20 minutes overnight on a separate branch.
Pair an attacker loop (every 3 hours) with a defender loop (every 20 minutes) and give the attacker a checklist plus a "do not make stuff up" rule.
Prompt the defender to make "structural changes" instead of fixing one link at a time, or it will under-fix.
Constrain research loops to official product-maker sources — Claude cannot reliably distinguish a press release from a Reddit comment.
Run three separate loops (import, research, verification) for time-sensitive data like crypto airdrops, with ~10-minute import intervals.
Accept that "thinking in loops" is a learned skill — design imperfect loops first, refine after observing hallucination patterns.

title: Tips and Tricks for Vibe Coding Websites
summary_verdict: A 24-hour WordPress-to-Astro rebuild powered by Claude for branding docs and MiniMax-M3 for execution worked, but the approach burns real money — ~800M tokens in a day and roughly $200/mo of hosting savings only offsets part of it.
summary_key_takeaways: Write two design docs first — a **branding guideline** and an **image guideline** — by pasting screenshots into Claude or GPT-4-class models before touching any code.
Use Opus or Claude for the two design docs, then drop to **MiniMax-M3 inside the Claude Code harness** for implementation to cut cost roughly 10x.
Burn the first M3 pass — it will re-skin your existing components. Recover by issuing explicit `match it one-to-one the theme, the style` instructions on the second pass.
Set a 24-hour revamp budget; expect ~800M tokens for a 1,000+ article site, which means upgrading past any sub-$200/mo plan to **Max + API credits**.
Migrate from WordPress to **Astro** if you want agents to refactor freely — WordPress is too tangled to "unleash AI on it."
Skip Higgsfield/Claude Code Design for the initial revamp — save them for part-two pixel tuning on buttons and spacing.
Reference a concrete site you like (creator used claude.ai's pastel + wavy-line look) and pass that as a reference, not a vague mood — it sharpens the color and typography output.

title: AI built me a Business in 48 Hours (Online Directory)
summary_verdict: Directories are a legitimately profitable side hustle if you pick a niche where you already have an audience and contacts — Martin shipped a Hong Kong gym directory in a weekend using Hostinger Horizons + Kimi, but monetization only kicks in once you reach 20–30 listings and start selling featured placements.
summary_key_takeaways: Pick a directory niche where you already have an unfair advantage — Martin's is Hong Kong gyms because his brother owns a gym and he's affiliated with several; yours should be a vertical where you know the owners personally.
Use the `8 versions + 5 judges` prompt pattern (skeptical CEO, distracted founder, competitor, ideal customer, conversion copywriter) to force AI to self-verify copy before it ever hits the page.

Don't single-shot the AI build — first ask Horizons for a prompt, feed that prompt into Kimi to generate a PRD, then return the PRD to Horizons. The first-pass build was visibly worse than the two-step version.

Hold paid listings until you have at least 20–30 free listings; Martin's monetization roadmap is free scrapes → free featured slots for big brands → paid featured slots for trainers and boutique gyms → AdSense → digital products.
Use Porkbun for domains and connect it to your VPS so your agent can serve the site — explicitly called out as the cheapest option the Boxmining office uses.
Plan a weekly cadence: Martin intends to launch a second directory in a different niche (pets, chiropractors, dental) the following week — treat directories as a portfolio, not a one-off.
Add a vibe-coded utility page like the 5-minute "Pick My Workout" roulette to make the directory feel interactive and shareable, not just a static list.

title: Why You MUST Secure Your Hermes Agent And How to Do It
summary_key_takeaways: The simplest Hermes agent deployment option exposes the dashboard to the public internet by default, making it the riskiest setup if left unsecured.
An unprotected dashboard allows attackers on the network to sniff transmitted data and attempt brute force attacks to gain unauthorized access.
A successful breach can give attackers access to API keys, configuration files, and the ability to execute shell commands acting as the legitimate agent owner.
The recommended solution is to place the dashboard behind Tailscale, a free VPN service that restricts access to authenticated devices on a private network.
Using Tailscale is described as the 'free method' for securing the deployment, making robust security accessible without additional cost.
VPN or Tailscale protection is non-negotiable for this deployment method—without it, the agent is fundamentally vulnerable to remote takeover.

title: How to Connect Hermes Agent VPS to Desktop App (FREE Guide)
summary_verdict: Worth attempting only as a local-dev convenience on Tailscale — the connection works but the desktop app bleeds tokens, lacks a file browser, and may drop within minutes of logout, so keep your VPS agent on TUI.
summary_key_takeaways: Use Tailscale — never expose the Hermes dashboard port (default 9191) directly to the public internet, or API keys and shell access are one brute-force away.
Stay on the username/password method only if you're on the Nuki **basic** plan; if you upgrade to the **plus** tier, switch to OAuth and skip the VPN overhead.
Pin desktop app to **version 0.16** or newer — older builds show a `session token` field and will not render the gateway sign-in form.
Keep the username as `admin` and `chmod 600` the `.env` file after the heredoc closes with `EOF`, so the OIDC secret isn't world-readable.

Persist the dashboard with `tmux new -s hermes -d 'hermes dashboard --no-open --bind 100.x.x.x:9191 …'` — expect a roughly 50% chance it survives a logout, so don't rely on it for unattended 24/7 use.
Keep coding/terminal sessions on the TUI client — the desktop app dilutes tokens with UI-schema overhead, costing you budget on every prompt.
Accept the missing file browser: the desktop sidebar shows the local filesystem, not the VPS, so use Termius SFTP for remote file work.

summary_key_takeaways: Run the desktop app only for project overviews; stay in the TUI for any task where token cost matters, since the desktop UI is not token-efficient.
Do not rely on the desktop app for MCP-heavy workflows until the open PR for desktop MCP discoverability lands.
Use the web dashboard's new system tab for messaging channel, MCP catalog, webhook, and credential changes instead of editing config files by hand.
Install Hermes with the blank-slate option if you do not want built-in skills, and keep the Skills Hub's `Nvidia skills` source enabled only if you actually use CUDA-X or NIM.
Treat the new `undo` command as your primary correction tool — it rolls back several user turns, which is faster than re-prompting.
Watch your read-file token usage; the `14%` reduction is automatic, but combine it with the new prompt-size diagnostic to catch context bloat early.

Rethink your prompting workflow — Anthropic's Boris Cherny and Peter Steinberger both argue you should `build a system that prompts itself` rather than prompt coding agents directly, which matches Hermes' uncapped spawn depth and `/goal` loop changes in v0.16.

title: STOP mixing your AI projects all at once, DO THIS instead
Tips to get better at Using Hermes Agent (Live Tutorial)
summary_verdict: Your Hermes agent going dumb is almost always a workspace and context problem, not a model problem — wipe the VPS, reinstall the OS, set up project folders with their own `agents.md`, and feed the official docs before you do anything else.
summary_key_takeaways: Reinstall the VPS OS before you touch Hermes config when a long-running agent starts hallucinating — it takes minutes and removes months of accumulated state in the Ubuntu directory.
Always create one `workspace` directory with its own Git repo as a sibling to the runtime `.hermes` folder, never run multi-project work inside a single chat session.
Put one `agents.md` in every project folder and one in the Ubuntu directory — the top-level file is architecture, the project files are conventions, mirroring `cloud.md` in Cloud Code.
Paste the full Hermes docs and the GitHub repo into the agent's context on every fresh setup, and instruct it to source troubleshooting info `only` from official documentation to cut hallucinations.
Run the agent in verbose mode so you can verify it's actually doing `file extract` and `web search` against the docs instead of guessing — if you don't see those calls, the habit hasn't stuck.
If you're migrating from Open Claw to Hermes Agent on an existing VPS, expect `a lot slower` performance until you reinstall the OS; don't try to debug in place.

title: How to Use MiniMax M3 in Claude Code (Setup Guide)
summary_verdict: Setting up MiniMax M3 inside Claude Code takes under 10 minutes and works today, but you're stuck at a 200K context window until MiniMax updates its Anthropic-compatible endpoint.
summary_key_takeaways: Use `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code` with the China mirror for speed, but expect this to break when Anthropic finishes deprecating the npm install path.
Run `unset ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` and grep `~/.bashrc` before configuring MiniMax, or Claude Code will silently route back to Anthropic's endpoint.

Treat the 200K context window as the hard ceiling for now — don't plan multi-file refactors that rely on MiniMax M3's advertised 1M context, since the Anthropic-compatible config block doesn't expose it yet.
Set your base URL to `minimaxi.com/anthropic` only if you're in China; everyone else should use the default MiniMax endpoint to avoid regional routing issues.
Always `cd` into the specific project directory before launching `claude` — running it from `$HOME` causes the model to audit unrelated files and wastes context.
Toggle verbose mode with `Ctrl+O` on every session, because MiniMax M3's think blocks contain the reasoning you'll need when reviewing its audit reports.
Add the MiniMax Coding Plan MCP only if your work involves live API research or reading UI screenshots — skip it for pure backend code to keep startup fast.

title: Hermes Agent MCP Catalog Guide (NEW)
summary_verdict: Hermes' built-in MCP catalog is an underrated, safer on-ramp for new users — but the current snapshot only ships Linear and n8n, so power users can skip it until GitHub lands.
summary_key_takeaways: Run `hermes update` before `hermes mcp` — catalog manifests are never auto-synced, so out-of-date snapshots will hide newly approved servers.
Stick to `hermes mcp install` for your first MCPs and only branch into hand-edited `config.yaml` files once you understand the tool-whitelist model.
Whitelist individual tools at install time and re-run `hermes mcp configure <name>` to revoke them later — never expose the full surface of an MCP server.
Treat any server outside the `optional/mcps` directory as unvetted; the catalog exists specifically because "some of them might not be safe" and users have been attacked.
For Linear, store OAuth tokens in `~/.hermes/mcp_tokens/linear.json`; for n8n, the base URL and API key go in `~/.hermes/.env` and Hermes probes the instance automatically.
Wait on the GitHub MCP until it ships — it's already listed in the manifest but disabled, and install is expected to take a personal access token scoped to your repos.
If you already run a custom MCP setup, skip the catalog — Ron calls it an update "more so for the everyday users, the casual users, the beginners."

title: Hermes Agent NEW Skill Bundles (Setup Guide)
summary_verdict: Skill Bundles are a real workflow fix for Hermes — group skills once, invoke with one slash command instead of hoping the agent picks all five subtasks from one prompt.

Hermes Agent Kanban Setup Guide (Multi-Agent Task Board)
summary_verdict: Hermes' Kanban task board is a real upgrade over blind sub-agent delegation for multi-step work, but the setup is annoyingly manual and you must create each assignee profile yourself — start with Story 1 before attempting Stories 2–4.
summary_key_takeaways: Run `hermes update` first — the Kanban release was ~8 hours old at recording, so older builds won't have the `hermes gateway start`, `hermes profile create`, or `hermes kanban` commands.

Create one Hermes profile per assignee with `hermes profile create <role>` (e.g. `researcher`, `backend-dev`); the parent agent's API key is NOT inherited, so set `inference_provider` and the key on every profile under `~/.hermes/profiles/<name>/`.
Resume a Kanban profile's TUI session with `hermes --profile <name> --resume <session_id>` — the default `hermes sessions list` only shows the main agent's sessions.

title: He Built 4 AI Influencers with AI Agents To Post Content 24//7 (Full Breakdown)
summary_verdict: AI agents running 24/7 faceless content accounts is a real, repeatable workflow — but the $10,000 in two months is from one operator's portfolio, not a single bot, and X is actively punishing automated posts.
summary_key_takeaways: Start with a single account, not four — Martin runs four profiles, but revenue is collective ($10,000), so per-account yield is closer to $2,500 over two months, not a single-bot payday.

Default to Threads for automated posting, not X — Martin explicitly states "X is cracking down on bot posts" while "threads is the one that you can really freely publish without worrying about the algorithm punishing you."
Route X and Instagram drafts through Buffer rather than posting direct from OpenClaw, since Buffer's internal AI handles timing for "the most organic reach."
Skip the SOUL.md context-blasting phase — Martin never configured one and got called out by Michael weeks later as actually using "the correct way."
Feed the agent canonical marketing books (Influence, Hormozi) rather than hand-writing a system prompt, mirroring Martin's "let your AI bot read all the books" approach.
Wait at least a few days of analytics before tuning — Martin only reconfigures after "x amount of posts" once the graphs move, not after every post.

Treat this as a 2-month build, not a weekend one — Martin calls it "six months or like see some real traction" for a human team, and even with agents he needed the full two-month window to reach $10,000.

title: Openclaw + Notion (EASY Setup Guide and Use Cases)
summary_verdict: Notion is the most underrated, free second-brain and data-analysis backend for your OpenClaw agent — the 5-minute setup is worth doing today.
summary_key_takeaways: Create the integration at `notion.so/profile` under `Internal Integrations` and copy the `Internal Integration Secret` — never paste it into Discord or WhatsApp.
Store the key in your `.env` as `NOTION_API_KEY=` via terminal (`Ctrl+X`, save buffer, Enter) instead of hardcoding it into chat channels.
Connect a Notion database by clicking the top-right `•••` → `Connections` → pick your integration; toggle off `Update content` and `Insert content` if you only want read access.
Offload journals and reports to Notion so the agent can run `semantic search` later instead of stuffing everything into its memory files.
Use a read-only Notion database to query large historical datasets — the creator's setup analyzed `548 trades over two years` and surfaced that `365` closed in under an hour.
Leave both `Update content` and `Insert content` enabled only if you want the agent to create or modify databases on its own — otherwise restrict to read-only.
Skip paid second-brain tools: Notion is free, database-native, and already what your agent needs for structured data analysis.

title: Building Boxmining AI overnight
summary_verdict: OpenClaw's agent mode is a genuine rapid-prototyping tool for solo creators — Banner built a full Windows 95-themed site in 8 hours from a midnight shower idea — but bug-fixing and iteration still require manual engineering work, so don't expect it to ship a finished product.
summary_key_takeaways: Use a pre-loaded agent like Banner that already has your channel, comments, and video context — don't run fresh `vibe coding` sessions on new projects.
Write prompts as intent plus cadence, not specs — e.g. `build like start, cron jumps every hour, evaluate, proceed at your own discretion.`
Cap AI scope to rapid prototyping only; never ask the same agent to fix bugs after the initial build, expect icons to `get combined together` after a resize request.
Schedule overnight AI build windows (the Boxmining site shipped in `8 hours` from a `12 a.m.` prompt) to test shower-thought ideas before they die.
Leave room for unprompted additions — Banner added `Minesweeper` and `Clippy` without being asked, and those were retained.
Test the live prototype at `boxminingai.com` and submit feedback on whether the Windows 95 nav beats the current `set of guides, troubleshooting, updates` layout.
Keep a manual engineering loop ready for the iteration phase; AI handles the 0→1, you handle the 1→N.

title: I BUILT an AI Food Tracking App Using Just ONE TOOL (OpenClaw)
summary_verdict: OpenClaw is the right tool when off-the-shelf nutrition apps don't fit a specific medical case — for the creator's fatty liver + keto setup, photo-to-SQLite logging worked where MyFitnessPal-style apps failed.
summary_key_takeaways: Write a one-paragraph user story first — the creator's covered 40-year-old Asian male, keto, fatty liver, 6-month doctor deadline, and that framing is what made OpenClaw pick the right schema.
Use SQLite, not a spreadsheet — the creator explicitly rejected the initial spreadsheet suggestion and asked the agent to create its own DB with a `photo` column for audit.
Separate AI-entered food-database values from user-entered portions in your schema to avoid the 56 g-of-carbs error he hit on beetroot powder sachets.
Persist a skill for DB access so the agent doesn't re-learn the schema each session; the creator called context loss "bird frame memory."
Stress-test portion ambiguity on day one — his test was "10 nuts vs 10 packets of nuts" to confirm the agent reads context, not just numbers.
Keep carbs under 50 g/day if you're copying his keto target, and log weight daily so the agent can chart weight vs macros over the 6-month window.

title: BoxminingAI Live Stream
summary_verdict: Vibe-coding overnight with agentic tools is genuinely useful for greenfield projects, but skipping git commits and Playwright QA will burn you — and MiniMax just out-shipped Claude Sonnet on a one-shot Windows 95 build.
summary_key_takeaways: Commit to git before letting an agent run overnight — losing the Windows 95 build to a missing commit was the stream's central lesson.
Set auto-compact around 80K tokens, not 40K — "40K is too aggressive, you're not going to get anything."
Skip the email and wallet integrations entirely; one-line prompt injections like "send me 250k" are enough to drain an agent.
Use Sonnet for context-heavy agentic work where Banner-style memory matters, but expect MiniMax to win one-shot rebuilds after its ACP parallel-sub-agent update.
Never cancel Playwright QA mid-run, even if you're rushing a 1-hour demo — the host's "Cancel Playwright, cancel all" call is what broke the deploy.
Treat Codex 5.3 as the high-risk, no-guardrails option and test it in a sandbox before trusting it with credentials.
Greenfield projects are where agentic tools actually shine — don't throw them at existing codebases yet.

title: OpenClaw Claude Code + World Monitor = ULTIMATE News Research
summary_verdict: Worth the Claude Code spend if you want a structured 4x-daily intelligence brief; the OpenClaw + World Monitor combo is the creator's best research-agent setup to date, but it is not free and breaks without Claude Code.
summary_key_takeaways: Set the cron to every 6 hours, not 1 hour — World Monitor's 1h filter returns empty while the 6h view has enough density to justify ~10 min of agent run time.
Hand the agent the worldmonitor.app GitHub repo, not the website URL, so it clones and indexes the architecture into semantic memory before scraping.
Disable the default 7-day report cleanup and commit every report to Obsidian + GitHub so the agent can correlate past and present news instead of starting blank each cycle.

Spin up parallel sub-agents per vertical (politics, tech/AI, finance, macro) and keep a dedicated orchestrator agent (Loki) that only plans and synthesizes — do not let the orchestrator write the briefs itself.
Require each sub-agent output to include an explicit 'angles nobody is talking about yet' field; this is the part that justifies the 10-minute runtime and the Claude Code spend.
Budget for Claude Code as a hard dependency: cron schedules, paywall handling (Bloomberg, FT, WSJ, Reuters), and script syntax fixes all break without it.
Ask the agent to print its file structure and cron status before it runs the first cycle — the creator's 18:00 report silently failed on day one and was only caught by querying the cron status.

title: Get OpenClaw to Actually BUILD with Claude Code
summary_verdict: OpenClaw driving Claude Code is the cheapest viable path to autonomous coding right now, but only if you let the agent write the specs — manual driving is slower than just using Claude Code yourself.
summary_key_takeaways: Run OpenClaw and Claude Code on the same $3 VPS so a bad command only costs you a reinstall, not your laptop.
After every setup, prompt the agent to debug Claude Code's own termination errors — that single check is what makes the loop stable.
Always enable `dangerously skip permissions` on the VPS instance; leave it off anywhere you keep real data.
Skip Cursor and Windsurf for spec-heavy work — make Claude Code write the spec, then let OpenClaw hand it off.
Swap in the `minimax.code` base URL trick if you only have the $20 MiniMax coding plan; the creator says it covers full builds.
Use the cron + Claude Code pattern (see Grandmaster) for any project that needs hourly regeneration, not just one-off builds.
When the model drifts, re-state the user requirement verbatim — don't fix the symptom, the spec is usually the bug.

title: OpenClaw Telegram Setup guide
summary_verdict: Worth the 5-minute setup for the streaming feedback on long agent tasks, but expect inconsistent chunked output on a Mac terminal and don't trust the agent to wire it up for you — the CLI path is the only reliable route.
summary_key_takeaways: Pin OpenClaw to **3.2** with `openclaw update` before touching channels, or the wizard will misbehave.
Use `openclaw config` → channels → telegram, not the agent's natural-language setup — the 2% failure mode kills the daemon.
After `/start`, expect a name prompt from the bot; answer it or override with "please pair it now" to force the pairing code.
Flip Telegram streaming from `partial` to `on` via the agent if Mac/Termius shows chunked output instead of real-time typing.
Assume `delete config` will be needed at least once; back up the token before wiping the wizard state.
Skip streaming for short replies — the creator's testing confirms it only visibly helps on 40-line haiku-or-larger generations.
Termius on iPad works for the SSH leg; don't rely on the in-agent CLI helper to handle BotFather for you.

title: How to Build an AI AGENT TEAM That RUNS YOUR BUSINESS for $3 month
summary_verdict: Specialized $3 OpenClaw agents coordinated by one orchestrator beat a single overloaded assistant, and Stark is the only one worth talking to — just give him server credentials and let him spawn the rest.
summary_key_takeaways: Run each agent on a separate $3/month Zebra VPS — never share one agent across multiple humans, or context bleed will make it "forgetful."
Give Stark the IP, username, password, and Discord token for each new server and let him SSH in to install OpenClaw and copy his own skill/memory files instead of provisioning manually.
Use the spawn prompt verbatim: "Copy your memory relevant to [project] to [new agent]. Copy your relevant skills and memory you think are important" — it works for moving state between bots.
Specialize aggressively: one bot per data source (Banner = YouTube API + transcripts + comment scan + topic proposal), and give it its own database instead of dumping tables into the orchestrator.
Skip Polymarket bots — the team's 3-day, full-strategy Ralph loop test topped out at a 55% win rate, and a friend's 92% win rate over 300 trades made $5 on $10 of capital.
Tell the orchestrator to write a dashboard of which agents it manages, so you don't have to track 8 OpenClaw instances in your head.
Don't put side projects on your main orchestrator — it was "overloading" Stark until the project moved to Grandmaster, a bot with Jeff Goldblum's personality and a scoped toolset.

title: Kimi Agent Swarm LIVE Review
summary_verdict: Don't pay for Kimi Agent Swarm on the $40/mo Allegro plan — it's a poorly executed first iteration, slower than open-source sub-agent setups, and refunds aren't an option once a run starts.
summary_key_takeaways: Skip the $40/mo Kimi Allegro upgrade — sub-agent orchestration in OpenClaw or Claude Code is free and faster.
Never start a 3-credit Agent Swarm run without a finalized prompt; there is no refund and no Escape-to-refine flow once it begins.
Migrate any routine task to OpenClaw's built-in sub-agent mode before paying Kimi, especially research-and-presentation workflows that need post-hoc tweaks.
Use Kimi Agent Swarm only if you have zero agent tooling — and even then, evaluate Perplexity Computer first for similar UI with stronger research output.
Reassess Kimi Agent Swarm in roughly three months; the creator's explicit call is to "let Kimi cook" and revisit once templates and ordering bugs are fixed.
Cap monthly Kimi spend by budgeting credits at the 1-credit-per-Claw-call baseline, since a single swarm run consumes three of those units in one shot.

title: Perplexity Computer Just KILLED Claude Code (Side-by-Side Test)
summary_verdict: Perplexity Computer is a better default than Claude Code for non-technical users who want quick, parallel research-and-build workflows, but Claude Code remains the pick if you want process control and already pay for it.
summary_key_takeaways: Start with the Pro plan only if you want search; pay $176/mo for the Max plan to unlock Perplexity Computer — the creator says Pro users are locked out, calling it "the scammy part."
Force Claude Code into plan mode and explicitly request agent teams before any multi-step build, or it "tried to brute force it" like it did in the side-by-side test.
Use Perplexity Computer's parallel orchestration for time-boxed research tasks (e.g. weekly stock monitoring) where the cron-style follow-up saves you from re-prompting.
Skip Perplexity Computer if you're already on Claude Max at $200/mo — the $24/mo savings does not offset losing process visibility on agent runs.
Pull live data through Perplexity Finance when building dashboards (the Hamptonism Bloomberg terminal clone does this) instead of wiring your own data sources.
Budget ~3 hours of manual Claude Code configuration (plan mode + sub-agents) to match a single Perplexity Computer run, then decide if that time is worth more than $30/mo.
Wait for a Pro-plan unlock or a public API before committing — the product is 6 hours old at filming and gated to Max only.

title: OpenClaw + API Guide (Step-by-Step)
summary_verdict: Worth the watch only as a workflow demo — the process works, but the live recording exposes real failure modes (Cloudflare blocks, hallucinations, language switches) that the creators barely address.
summary_key_takeaways: Use youtubetranscripts.io on the free plan as the reference API when teaching OpenClaw any new external fetch — it's the only concrete service named end-to-end.
Save API tokens to the OpenClaw `.env` file rather than pasting them into chat history, since the bot is `trained not to reveal what's in the environmental folder`.
Paste the full API documentation page into the chat and instruct the bot to `read up on this API documentation and make a skill` — do not paraphrase the docs yourself.
Build every API integration as a named skill after a successful test, so the bot auto-uses it on future prompts (Stark completed the transcript task with zero extra instructions once trained).
When a run stalls, start a new session instead of continuing — the creators explicitly recommend this when Banner looped on a Cloudflare block and started replying in Chinese.
Verify every API result by re-running the same query, because Opus-class agents `can sometimes deliver results incorrectly` and may claim success on hallucinated output.
Expect Cloudflare blocks when pasting docs as Markdown — switch to uploading the documentation as a file attachment if the inline paste gets flagged.

title: My OpenClaw is STUPID (Here's how to Fix It)
summary_verdict: OpenClaw's failures are a prompting problem, not a model problem — break tasks down, test each step, and save working workflows as skills.
summary_key_takeaways: Give OpenClaw the API documentation first and say "Learn and understand this first" before any data calls.
Ask the agent to explicitly "test the connection" and screenshot the result before declaring a build done.
Push intermediate data into a vector database so later steps can query it instead of re-browsing 30+ sources.
After a workflow runs cleanly once, tell OpenClaw to "save this as a skill for future reference" to lock in the steps.
Start with a small task, confirm it works, then scale — simpler tasks fail more often than complex ones in the host's experience.
Never grant root access to a messaging agent; the cited Facebook/Meta case messaged the entire contact list.
Demand raw artifacts like "give me the tweets" or a dashboard link to catch hallucinated outputs in real time.

title: KimiClaw Setup Guide (Openclaw on Kimi 2.5)
summary_verdict: Kimi Claw is "very mid" — the $40/month Allegro plan buys a one-minute setup but no real features over a $2 self-hosted OpenClaw pointing at Kimi 2.5 via OpenRouter.
summary_key_takeaways: Skip Kimi Claw if you already self-host OpenClaw — the $40/month Allegro plan only buys convenience, not features.
Use OpenRouter's Kimi 2.5 endpoint (~$0.50/M input, $2/M output) and wire it into your own OpenClaw on a $2/month Zeabur VPS to skip the wrapper entirely.
Enable agent swarm via Kimi Claw only if you need the orchestrator pattern out of the box — it's the single feature not trivially replicated on a self-hosted box.
Assume Moonshot retains prompt data — the creator flags the trust gap with a Chinese-hosted service, so don't send anything sensitive.
Don't expect X/Twitter access — the Kimi Claw agent has no X API, so social-sentiment workflows still need manual workarounds.
Watch for Kimi-specific features in future updates — the creator expects Moonshot to add value beyond raw hosting or this stays uncompetitive.

title: Minimax 2.5 is MUCH better and I can PROVE it
summary_verdict: Upgrade to MiniMax 2.5 if you run agent workflows — the 2.5 model passes the car-wash logic test where 2.1 fails, and the $20 coding plan makes it far cheaper than Opus for everyday tasks.
summary_key_takeaways: Run the car-wash prompt ("I need to wash my car at the car wash. Should I walk or should I drive over?") on every model you use for agents — if it says walk, do not trust it for planning tasks.
Buy the $20 MiniMax coding plan (300 prompts / 5 hours) before paying Opus rates for routine agent work.
Reserve Opus for "mission critical" tasks only; expect ~$5/day in OpenClaw heartbeat charges on top of the $75 per 1M output tokens.
Skip fully-local Llama setups for agent orchestration — the creator says "most local models fail" at simple multi-step planning.
Use an Opus "manager" agent that delegates small-tool coding to MiniMax 2.5 to cut token spend without losing quality on big builds.
Re-run any MiniMax 2.5 answer at least twice — the creator saw it flip to the wrong "walk" answer on "one or two" out of several attempts.
Treat any specific dates or stats in agent-generated slides as unverified — the MiniMax 2.5 deck hallucinated a 2021 release date for the model itself.
